meta.framing:
You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 20 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

prior.knowledge: For pretraining a 200M‑parameter model on 100B tokens, learning‑rate ≈2×10⁻⁴ is empirically the lowest variance range; raising lr beyond 5×10⁻³ often yields validation‑loss spikes or instability.
prior.expected_structure: Validations will be numbers; best (lowest) loss ≈ 2.1e‑4 at lr=2.441e-4, bs=256, other combos > 5e‑4.
prior.approach: Begin with lr=2.441e-4 and a batch size that gives good GPU utilization (e.g., 256) to observe convergence; then increase lr stepwise up to 5.524e-3 while keeping bs=256, recording where loss plateaus, and finally test larger bs around 512 if plateau persists, suspecting insufficient data per batch.
uncertainties: Exact optimal (lr, bs) pair is unknown;
batch‑size does not heavily affect loss but may affect GPU memory constraints;
higher lr may cause premature termination or overflow rather than smooth loss.
